checks.a_analysis: I have carefully compared the output JSON against the source PDF pages 1 through 16. All body text in the output has a corresponding basis in the source document. No fabricated paragraphs, sentences, or fictional sections were found. The document is a transcript of a stakeholder meeting and the output faithfully records the spoken words as presented in the source.

The output contains one email address: 'Medicaid.Transformation@DHHS.NC.gov'. I verified this against page 16 of the source PDF, where it is listed in Janie Shaver's final comments. The email address matches exactly. No fabricated phone numbers, mailing addresses, or URLs were found.

I verified the content of every page (1-16) against the JSON structure. While the output bundles a significant amount of content from pages 11-16 into a single section (Section 15), all text content from the source is present. Page 1-2 (Kody Kinsley), Page 3-9 (Dave Richard), and Page 10-16 (Q&A and Closing) are all accounted for.
The source PDF contains no tables; it is an entirely text-based transcript. Therefore, this check does not apply.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I inspected long paragraphs and section breaks to ensure continuity. For instance, the transition between page 5 and 6 (within Slide 8 discussion) is preserved in Section 4, Paragraph 9. The Q&A sections correctly map individual speakers to their responses. No significant blocks of text or paragraphs were omitted.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis:
Several minor wording differences were found between the source PDF text and the output JSON, likely due to OCR errors. These changes alter specific terms in a way that changes meaning. 
1. On page 1, 'behavioral health policy' became 'behavioral health with policy'.
2. On page 10, 'regions' became 'reasons' in the first question.
3. On page 11, 'physical health' was converted to 'fiscal health' in the first paragraph.
4. On page 15, 'substance use disorder array' was converted to 'fitness use disorder array'.
checks.b_evidence_source:
...pick up additional coverage for physical health and pharmacy services...
In regards to the substance use disorder array of services...
...driving and designing the behavioral health policy for the state.
How will the Department decide on which two regions go first...
checks.b_evidence_output:
...pick up additional coverage for fiscal health and pharmacy services...
In regards to the fitness use disorder array of services...
...driving and designing the behavioral health with policy for the state.
How will the Department decide on which two reasons go first...
checks.b_evidence_location: Reading Order 9 (p. 11), Section 15 Para under Kelsey (p. 15), Section 4 Para 3 (p. 1), Section 5 Para 2 (p. 10)

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain duplicated text blocks beyond what is intentionally present in the source (e.g., repeating speaker names in a transcript). Each speaker's distinct address is represented once in the logical flow.

The heading hierarchy is severely compromised in the latter half of the document. First, multiple speaker headings ('Deb Goda') identified as bold text in the source (pages 12, 14, 15) are completely omitted in the output, leading to their speech paragraphs being structurally merged with the preceding speaker. Second, the document structure shifts from a flat series of speaker sections (Sections 2-14) to a nested model where all speakers from page 12 onwards are incorrectly represented as H3 elements inside a single 'Deb Goda' section (Section 15), creating a false and inconsistent hierarchical relationship.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 12: 'Deb Goda' (heading for 'Psychological testing...'); Page 14: 'Deb Goda' (heading for 'That will continue...'); Page 15: 'Deb Goda' (heading for 'The CDSA services...')
checks.b_evidence_output: The output for Section 15 omits these speaker headings entirely. Additionally, multiple independent speakers (Debra, Kelsey, Dave Richard, Dr. McCoy, Janie Shaver) are incorrectly nested as H3 elements under Section 15 ('Deb Goda') instead of being treated as peer sections.

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no tables.

The reading order follows the source document's flow from top to bottom, page by page. Although structural delimiters (headings) are missing or incorrectly nested in the latter half, the narrative sequence of the transcript is preserved.
The source document contains no structured lists (numbered or bulleted).
The email address found on the final page of the source is correctly captured as a mailto link in the output.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF is a standard single-column text transcript organized by speaker headings (e.g., Janie Shaver, Kody Kinsley). The HTML output faithfully replicates this visual organization, maintaining the single-column flow and the relationship between speaker headings and their respective dialogue. The addition of a Table of Contents is an expected navigation feature and does not disrupt the visual organization of the primary content.
A thorough review of all 16 pages of the source PDF confirms that there are no tables present in the document. Therefore, there are no tables to be misrendered in the HTML output.

The source PDF is composed entirely of text and does not contain any meaningful images, logos, charts, or diagrams. The HTML output correctly reflects this by providing the text content without missing image placeholders.

The HTML output shows a sequential rendering of the transcript text matching the source PDF. There is no evidence of body content duplication, such as repeated paragraphs or sections. The repetition of speaker names in the Table of Contents versus the body headings is an intentional and accepted accessibility feature.
